Anti Competitive Agreements.
Both Vertical and Horizontal Agreements

Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India, (1978) 1 SCC 248, is a landmark judgment delivered by a 7-judge Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court of India that significantly expanded the scope of fundamental rights under the Constitution. The case arose when Maneka Gandhi’s passport was impounded by the government “in public interest” without providing her a reason or an opportunity to be heard. She challenged this action as a violation of her fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21.

The Supreme Court, in a transformative ruling, held that the “procedure established by law” under Article 21 must be “right, just, and fair” and not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. The Court established the interrelationship between Articles 14, 19, and 21, thereby introducing the doctrine that any law depriving a person of personal liberty must pass the tests of all three Articles. This decision marked a shift from a narrow interpretation of personal liberty to a broader, more substantive due process approach, strengthening the protection of individual rights against arbitrary state action.

Union of India, (1978) 1 SCC 248, is a landmark judgment delivered by a 7-judge Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court of India that significantly expanded the scope of fundamental rights under the Constitution. The case arose when Maneka Gandhi’s passport was impounded by the government “in public interest” without providing her a reason or an opportunity to be heard. She challenged this action as a violation of her fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21.

Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India, (1978) 1 SCC 248, is a landmark judgment delivered by a 7-judge Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court of India that significantly expanded the scope of fundamental rights under the Constitution. The case arose when Maneka Gandhi’s passport was impounded by the government “in public interest” without providing her a reason or an opportunity to be heard. She challenged this action as a violation of her fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21.

The Supreme Court, in a transformative ruling, held that the “procedure established by law” under Article 21 must be “right, just, and fair” and not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. The Court established the interrelationship between Articles 14, 19, and 21, thereby introducing the doctrine that any law depriving a person of personal liberty must pass the tests of all three Articles. This decision marked a shift from a narrow interpretation of personal liberty to a broader, more substantive due process approach, strengthening the protection of individual rights against arbitrary state action.

Anti Competitive Agreements.
Both Vertical and Horizontal Agreements

Section 3 of the Competition Act, 2002 deals with **anti-competitive agreements**. It provides that any agreement between enterprises, persons, or associations that causes or is likely to cause an **appreciable adverse effect on competition (AAEC)** within India is prohibited. Such agreements are considered **void**, meaning they have no legal enforceability. The objective of this provision is to ensure that markets remain fair, competitive, and beneficial for consumers.

The section broadly covers two types of agreements: **horizontal agreements** and **vertical agreements**. Horizontal agreements are those entered into between competitors operating at the same level of the market. These include practices such as price fixing, limiting production or supply, market sharing, and bid rigging. Such agreements are presumed to have an adverse effect on competition and are generally considered illegal without the need for detailed analysis because they directly harm market competition.

Vertical agreements, on the other hand, occur between enterprises at different stages of the supply chain, such as manufacturers and distributors. These include arrangements like tie-in agreements, exclusive supply or distribution agreements, refusal to deal, and resale price maintenance. Unlike horizontal agreements, vertical agreements are not automatically presumed to be illegal. Instead, they are assessed on a case-by-case basis to determine whether they actually cause an appreciable adverse effect on competition.

The enforcement of Section 3 is carried out by the Competition Commission of India, which investigates alleged violations and determines whether an agreement has adversely affected competition. If a violation is found, the Commission can impose penalties, direct modification of agreements, or order cessation of such practices.

Overall, Section 3 plays a crucial role in maintaining competitive markets in India by preventing collusion and restrictive trade practices. It protects consumer interests, promotes efficiency, and ensures that businesses compete fairly rather than manipulate the market through agreements.

Section 4 of the Competition Act, 2002 prohibits the abuse of a dominant position by an enterprise or group, though it does not prohibit dominance itself. A dominant position refers to a position of strength in the relevant market that enables an enterprise to operate independently of competitive forces or to affect competitors or consumers in its favor. Abuse occurs when such an enterprise engages in practices such as imposing unfair or discriminatory conditions or prices (including predatory pricing), limiting production, supply, or technical development, denying market access to competitors, making contracts subject to supplementary obligations unrelated to the main transaction, or leveraging its dominance in one market to enter or protect another market. The determination of dominance depends on factors like market share, size, resources, and the relevant product and geographic market.

Section 4 of the Competition Act, 2002, prohibits the abuse of a dominant position by any enterprise or group in India. It does not penalize holding a dominant position itself, but rather the abuse of such market power to restrict competition, exploit consumers, or hinder market access. The CCI identifies abuse through unfair pricing (including predatory pricing), limiting production, or imposing, as shown on Taxmann and iPleaders, unfair conditions/restrictions. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]  
Key Aspects of Abuse of Dominant Position (Section 4) 

• Definition of Dominance: A dominant position is a position of strength that allows an enterprise to operate independently of competitive forces or affect competitors/consumers in its favor. 
• Prohibited Conduct (Abuse): 

	• Exploitative/Discriminatory Practices: Imposing unfair/discriminatory conditions or prices (including predatory pricing) in the sale or purchase of goods/services. 
	• Limiting Production/Technical Development: Restricting production, markets, or technical/scientific development to the prejudice of consumers. 
	• Market Access Denial: Engaging in practices resulting in denial of market access, such as exclusive dealing or tying, as noted on Lexology and discussed in the  Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp case 
. 
	• Leveraging: Using a dominant position in one market to enter or protect another relevant market. 
	• Supplementary Obligations: Making contracts subject to acceptance of additional, unrelated obligations, like in Kshitij Arya v. Google LLC. 

• Exceptions: 

	• Actions taken to meet competition, such as reasonable price differences based on cost or commercial justification, are generally not considered abuse, explained on  India Code 
. 

• Factors Determining Dominance: 

	• Market share, size/resources of the enterprise, competitor size, and consumer dependence. 

• Enforcement & Penalty: 

	• The  Competition Commission of India (CCI) 
 enforces Section 4. Violations can lead to severe penalties, such as the ₹936.44 crore penalty on Google for abusing its Play Store dominance, as detailed on  Taxmann 
.

[4] https://getswipe.in/blog/article/abuse-of-dominant-position-competition-law-explained
[5] http://www.manupatracademy.com/LegalPost/Abuse_of_Dominant_Position_in_Digital_Markets
[6] https://www.lawaudience.com/abuse-of-dominant-position-under-competition-act-2002/
[7] https://www.cyrilshroff.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/Abuse-of-Dominance-Vertical-Agreements.pdf
[8] https://www.taxmann.com/post/blog/prohibition-of-dominant-position-abuse
[9] https://lc2.du.ac.in/DATA/Abuse%20of%20Dominant%20Position.pptx
[10] https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=177d9d82-7abb-4c90-b01f-542c21687545
[11] https://www.scribd.com/document/517811168/abuse-of-dominance-position
[12] https://www.cbflnludelhi.in/post/the-precondition-of-dominance-under-section-4

Section 3 of the Competition Act, 2002 prohibits agreements between enterprises, persons, or associations that cause or are likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect on competition (AAEC) in India, and such agreements are declared void. It covers both horizontal and vertical agreements. Horizontal agreements, entered into between competitors operating at the same level of the market, include practices such as price fixing, limiting production or supply, market sharing, and bid rigging, and these are presumed to have an adverse effect on competition. Vertical agreements, entered into between parties at different levels of the supply chain (such as manufacturers and distributors), include tie-in arrangements, exclusive supply or distribution agreements, refusal to deal, and resale price maintenance; these are not presumed illegal but are assessed based on their actual impact on competition. However, reasonable conditions imposed for protecting intellectual property rights and export-related agreements are exempt under this section.

Section 4 of the Competition Act, 2002, prohibits the abuse of a dominant position by any enterprise or group in India. It does not penalize holding a dominant position itself, but rather the abuse of such market power to restrict competition, exploit consumers, or hinder market access. The CCI identifies abuse through unfair pricing (including predatory pricing), limiting production, or imposing, as shown on Taxmann and iPleaders, unfair conditions/restrictions. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]  
Key Aspects of Abuse of Dominant Position (Section 4) 

• Definition of Dominance: A dominant position is a position of strength that allows an enterprise to operate independently of competitive forces or affect competitors/consumers in its favor. 
• Prohibited Conduct (Abuse): 

	• Exploitative/Discriminatory Practices: Imposing unfair/discriminatory conditions or prices (including predatory pricing) in the sale or purchase of goods/services. 
	• Limiting Production/Technical Development: Restricting production, markets, or technical/scientific development to the prejudice of consumers. 
	• Market Access Denial: Engaging in practices resulting in denial of market access, such as exclusive dealing or tying, as noted on Lexology and discussed in the  Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp case 
. 
	• Leveraging: Using a dominant position in one market to enter or protect another relevant market. 
	• Supplementary Obligations: Making contracts subject to acceptance of additional, unrelated obligations, like in Kshitij Arya v. Google LLC. 

• Exceptions: 

	• Actions taken to meet competition, such as reasonable price differences based on cost or commercial justification, are generally not considered abuse, explained on  India Code 
. 

• Factors Determining Dominance: 

	• Market share, size/resources of the enterprise, competitor size, and consumer dependence. 

• Enforcement & Penalty: 

	• The  Competition Commission of India (CCI) 
 enforces Section 4. Violations can lead to severe penalties, such as the ₹936.44 crore penalty on Google for abusing its Play Store dominance, as detailed on  Taxmann 
.
